PM.Summary: This young porpoise had live stranded and was euthanased. It was in very good condition; it had a good blubber layer and the carcase was well rounded. There was haemorrhage in the anterior chambers of both eyes and some eyelid trauma. The porpoise had not been feeding for some time prior to death; there were erosions in the oesophagus and the stomachs and intestines were empty. There was a low lungworm burden but there was excess associated mucus in some airways. The only other pathological changes present were those associated with the live stranding process.  Comment:  Normally porpoises that live strand are obviously diseased.  This was not the case in this instance and there was no evidence of any underlying disease process to explain either the live stranding or why a good condition porpoise had stopped feeding. This suggests that other factors such as maternal separation may have been involved.

Cause.of.Death: starvation (sequel to gastric impaction, fish bones)

PM.Summary: This young porpoise was in good condition with a well rounded carcase and a good blubber layer.There were many bottlenose dolphin teeth rakes particularly on the back anterior to the dorsal fin, but they were also present on the flanks and fins.There was some sub-blubber separation and bruising. There were a few rib fractures on the right side, but the vertebral spinous processes, spine and back musculature were all intact.The airways were filled with thick, white froth.Comment:This porpoise had been attacked by a bottlenose dolphin or dolphins.   However, the injuries present in this case were less severe than usual and they would not have been immediately fatal.The cause of death was from drowning.

PM.Summary: This juvenile male harbour porpoise was in thin condition. There were extensive disseminated yellow lesions/ abscesses throughout the blubber subcutaneous tissue. There was brown purulent fluid/discharge in both trachea and bronchi this was accompanied by a  congestion in the lung parenchyma and disseminated abscessation in the mediastinum and emphysemas around the great vessels and rete mirabilis. There was notable dilation of the right ventricle of the heart indicating chronic pulmonary hypertension. There was severe diffuse lymphadenopathy particularly in the PALN. No large focal abscesses were found and all other organs appeared grossly normal. A haemolytic Staphylococcus sp. was isolated from the lung, lung fluid, subcutaneous lesion and the choroid plexus suggesting a metastatic spread resulting in a Staphylococcus pyaemia. Pending histopathology.

This juvenile female harbour porpoise was in a good too fat body condition and showed evidence of recent successful feeding. There was blunt trauma to the right flank resulting in ante-mortem fractures to scapula and to ribs 2 to 10 in the dorsal/proximal end in a line with the spinous processes. The spinous processes and spine were intact. The lungs were collapsed, very congested, and contained a significant amount of stable foam. Focal trauma to the right flank most likely preceded death, and stable foam and cerebral haemorrhage would be consistent with anoxia. Acute trauma from a blunt object and subsequent anoxia are therefore the likely cause of death and this would be consistent with an attack of bottlenose dolphins and subsequent shock. However no rake marks or net marks were seen (though examination may be hampered by avian scavenger damage). No isolates were recovered from any of the tissues cultured for bacteriology.

This juvenile male harbour porpoise showed convincing evidence of a fatal interaction with bottlenose dolphins. The impact had been so severe that the blubber was liquefied and there was severe subarticular haemorrhage around the scapula. All ribs were fractured bilaterally, and there was a single fracture to the spine. The animal had recently been feeding and was in a good body condition, with a low parasite burden and healthy/fat blubber layer and pleural fat. Death was acute. The isolation of Brucella ceti from the spleen and the Haemophilus-like organism from the lung are not thought to be significant in this case.

PM.Summary: This was a immature female harbour porpoise possibly, not yet weaned. It had numerous bottlenose dolphin rake marks over the tail stock and around the vent. It had fractured ribs and associated haemorrhage on both sides. The lungs appeared very congested and underinflated/ collapsed the significance of this is at present uncertain.  The cause of death was trauma due to bottlenose dolphin attack.

This Juvenile female harbour porpoise was reported dead stranded following a period of heavy seas and cold weather. There was evidence of historic BND interactions based on healing rake marks 11mm apart. No fractures were present. The parasite burden was low. There was no evidence of recent feeding, or evidence of severe catabolism based on the gross appearance of the liver. Bile reflux would indicate that feeding hadn‚Äôt taken place for some time. The significance of the Mycopalsma sp. isolate is uncertain.  Histopathologcial  examination revealed  mild, sub-acute to chronic, multi-focal verminous pneumonia with little inflammatory reaction and mild, chronic-active diffuse eosinophilic (verminous) lymphadenitis. The severity of these lesions are unlikely to have resulted in significant compromise of this animal and histopathology showed a  mild, sub-acute to chronic, multi-focal verminous pneumonia with little inflammatory reaction. Mild, chronic-active diffuse eosinophilic (verminous) lymphadenitis. This together with the thin blubber layer suggests starvation/hypothermia as the most probable cause of death.

This Juvenile harbour porpoise had traumatic injuries consistent with an attack by a bottlenose dolphin. Multiple rib fractures intracranial bleeding, multiple BND rake marks, and a punctured lung. Foam in the lungs would suggest death likely from anoxia at sea.

This juvenile female harbour porpoise had pathology present consistent with live stranding as a result of starvation hypothermia. The animal was in very thin condition but had evidence of some recent feeding with the remains of crustaceans and numerous otiliths present in the cardiac section of the stomach.  The animal showed no pathologies consistent with disease processes and there was no evidence of parasitism. There was no obvious evidence of liver catabolism. Bacteriology did not reveal any significant isolates. Evidence so far would suggest a thin animal that was failing to thrive and live stranded. Histopathology did not show any evidence of a clinically relevant infectious process present in the tissues examined. Cause of stranding most probably hypothermia as a result of poor blubber condition and insufficient nutritional intake.

This juvenile female harbour porpoise was observed being  possibly predated on by a seal at sea on the 8th of May. It drifted ashore and was recovered and secured away from any potential scavengers pending collection the following day. There was extensive evidence of bite marks, puncture wounds and tissue defects, most notably the near complete removal of the longissimus dorsi muscle from both side of the thoracic vertebrae. Of note were several micro haemorrhages associated with the puncture marks which suggest this was an attack on a live animal and not scavenging of a carcase. This would be consistent with grey seal predation cases seen elsewhere in the Netherlands.  There was some evidence of the observed avian scavenger damage, most notably over the ribs. There was no evidence of any underlying disease, the animal was in good condition and had recently fed.  The stable foam in the lung and water stomach contents would be consistent with anoxic drowning and possible aspiration and or ingestion of sea water.  The isolation of a Pastuerella sp. from the lung is thought to be an incidental finding. Histology  showed amild to moderate, chronic-active, multifocal verminous pneumonia and a mild to moderate, chronic bile duct hyperplasia. All these findings are well within the normal limits found in juvenile harbour porpoises.  Given the observed attack, seal predation is the most probable cause of death of this otherwise healthy animal.

This juvenile male harbour porpoise was in good condition with good blubber thickness. There was no evidence of recent feeding with all stomach chambers empty there was also some fatty change in the liver. Haemorrhage was present around both mandibles and over the pharynx. The bronchi contained stable foam and the lungs showed severe congestion. There was no visible evidence of parasitism in any system examined. All other organs appeared unremarkable. Bacteriology produced no significant isolates. Histology revealed only freeze/thaw artefacts. There is some evidence of trauma to the pharyngeal area and mandibles, the stable foam and congestion in the lungs indicating anoxia this is very suggestive of trauma due to bycatch. However there were no obvious net marks on the body

Cause.of.Death: Live stranded due to generalised bacterial infection leading to multiple abscessation.
PM.Summary: This juvenile harbour porpoise was found live stranded at White Ness Shetland. It was euthanased by a single 0.22 calibre shot to the head. The resulting fracture to the cranial vault and trauma to the brain suggests that death was almost instantaneous. There were numerous rake marks consistent with a bottlenose dolphin attack present on the abdomen, melon and dorsal fin. The blubber exhibited multiple diffuse abscessation and necrosis presenting as a yellow/grey nutmeg appearance to the blubber with dark, necrotic appearance to the underlying musculature. A large well encapsulated purulent sub lumber abscess containing around 500ml of turbid yellow/green fluid extended from the right side of the diaphragm to the tail peduncle. The abscess did not extend into the abdominal cavity and appeared chronic perhaps 2 to 3 weeks old. Given its location it would have likely impaired movement and given rise to ‚Äútoxic shock‚Äů. There was a second smaller purulent abscess below this in the peritoneal wall.  All other visceral organs appeared unremarkable and the was some evidence of recent feeding. The original cause of the abscessation is not clear as there was no evidence of a foreign body or fractures associated with either. However given the bottlenose dolphin rake marks it is possible that a previous traumatic encounter may be the underlying cause. No significant isolates were cultured from the smaller abscess however Aeromonas hydrophila was isolated along with an Acinetobacter sp and a Enterobacter sp. from the larger abscess the significance of these isolates is uncertain.

PM.Summary: This female harbour porpoise was in good body condition.  The encircling wounds, marks and notches in the left pectoral, tail flukes and mandible, the skin tag on the left tail fluke, the subcutaneous and intramuscular haemorrhages over the head and the presence of froth in the airways are consistent with a diagnosis of bycatch.

Cause.of.Death: parasitism, heavy (multiple sites) and physical trauma, acute

PM.Summary: The juvenile male porpoise was in good body condition and showed signs of recent feeding, however had a moderate to high parasite burden consisting of fluke lesions in the liver and pancreas and nematodes in the respiratory system. The lungs also appeared to have a secondary bacterial infection. There were, in addition to this evidence of infectious disease, indications of both ante and post mortem trauma. There were rake marks and areas of deep focal bruising in the blubber and to the rete, caudo-dorsally to the spinous processes of T7-8. There was also extensive trauma to the ribs, with multiple fractures, fractures to the dorsal spinous processes and the palate, however these did not have significant bruising associated with them so it is assumed were received after death.

The animal was in good body condition but had a severe pulmonary parasite burden. The stomach was empty with some gastric ulceration however it is likely the animal debilitated very quickly as blubber condition was good. The liver was fatty, possibly a result of acute starvation and/or steroid therapy. It is possible the magnitude of the parasite burden increased acutely based on the small size of some of the nematodes.

Location: Kingsnorth Power station, on the Medway.
Cause.of.Death: starvation (sequel to gastric impaction, fish bones)

PM.Summary: This juvenile male porpoise was in good condition and  appeared to be a live stranding case. There was no indication of systemic disease and the animal was in good condition but had acutely not been feeding. The stomach was empty apart from some sand eel otoliths and there was a small region of linear ulceration in the glandular stomach. A single rake mark, possibly from a BND was present over the dorsum cranial to the dorsal fin and it showed signs of granulation indicating it had occurred at least several days prior to death. There was a low parasite burden in the tissues and no indication of infectious disease apart from a slightly thickened margin to the cardiac valves.

Cause.of.Death: Maternal separation and subsequent live stranding
PM.Summary: A neonatal male porpoise with asymettric but bilaterally inflated lungs, good blubber layer and evidence of subcuticular bruising. Autolysis and freeze thaw artifacts however preclude a full necropsy, however based on the evidence maternal separation and subsequent live stranding is likely.

This was a juvenile male porpoise in very good body condition with some mild predation of the head.  There were some antemortem bite/scratch marks on epidermis, mainly around the the head but also on the flank. No bruising was evident and no typical BND rake marks were seen. There was no evidence of net marks and the lungs were  not hyperinflated. There was no stable foam noted in the lungs. There was evidence of recent feeding; otoliths , bones and scant fish residue were present in the cardiac stomach. There was no indication of any gross pathology suggesting infectious disease. A toxic cause of death cannot be ruled out, however a suspected bycatch is perhaps more likely
